Tafseer:
(When the hypocrites come to you, they say, 'We testify that you are the Messenger of Allah.' And Allah knows that you are His Messenger, and Allah testifies that the hypocrites are liars) (1).
(The character of deep-rooted hypocrites): A reference to the hypocritical behavior of a group was also made in the previous Surah, but there the matter was addressed indirectly using a general form. In this Surah, the veil is completely lifted, which indicates that it discusses the character of those hypocrites who had become so deep-rooted in hypocrisy that no hope remained for their reform. Consequently, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) further on, it is said that even if you ask forgiveness for them, Allah will never forgive them. In fact, in another place, it is even said that if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will still not forgive them.
It is stated that when these hypocrites come to you, O Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), they swear oaths and declare that you are the Messenger of Allah, but Allah also testifies that these hypocrites are absolute liars. We have clarified elsewhere that in Arabic, the words 'nashhadu' (we testify) and 'Wallahu yashhadu' (and Allah testifies) carry the meaning of an oath. That is, the hypocrites repeatedly swear oaths to assure the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) of their faith and submission. They feel the need to swear oaths because their continuous errors have compromised their credibility to such an extent that they themselves realize no one will believe them unless they swear an oath. A person who has confidence in his deeds does not swear oaths unnecessarily, but for one who lacks confidence in his deeds, oaths are his only support. For this reason, the attribute of one who swears oaths excessively is described in the Qur'an as 'maheen' (worthless), and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was instructed: (And do not obey every worthless habitual swearer) (Al-Qalam, 68:10).
(And Allah knows that you are His Messenger, and Allah testifies that the hypocrites are liars): This phrase is exceptionally eloquent and timely. It states that as far as your being His Messenger is concerned, it is a reality that does not depend on their testimony; Allah well knows that you are His Messenger, but Allah bears witness that these hypocrites are liars in this testimony of theirs.

(They have taken their oaths as a screen, so they averted [people] from the way of Allah. Indeed, evil is that which they used to do) (2).
(The fate of those who use oaths as a shield): However, they have made their oaths a shield to protect themselves from your accountability, so that despite the conspiracies and mischief they constantly carry out against Islam, you would have no doubts about them and they would continue to be regarded as sincere toward Islam. In Surah Al-Mujadilah, verse 14, this conspiracy of these hypocrites is described: they have cultivated friendship with the Jews, a people with whom Allah is angry. On one hand, they reassure them through their oaths that they are with them, and on the other hand, they also swear to the Muslims to reassure them that they are their companions. Following this, with a minor variation in wording, this same verse appears there as well, stating:
(They have taken their oaths as a cover, so they averted [people] from the way of Allah, and for them is a humiliating punishment.) (Al-Mujadilah, 58:16).
That is, behind this shield, they believe they have secured themselves from both the Jews and the Muslims, and thus they have halted the step they had taken in the way of Allah. However, this refuge will not protect them for long; rather, they will very soon face a humiliating punishment. (so they averted from the way of Allah - the word 'sadda') is used both intransitively and transitively. Here, the context indicates it is used intransitively. That is, they first took a step toward Islam but have now stopped behind the screen of their oaths. They assume that if they can establish their Islamic credentials and deceive the Muslims merely on the strength of oaths, why should they go further and invite risks? It is stated: (Indeed, evil is that which they used to do) - they consider this highly clever diplomacy, but it is not clever diplomacy; rather, it is an extremely foolish plot, the highly disastrous consequences of which will soon confront them.

(That is because they believed, then disbelieved; so their hearts were sealed, and they do not understand) (3).
It is stated that they chose this path because Allah has sealed their hearts, as a result of which they have been deprived of the blessing of sound intellect. The reason for this seal is that after accepting faith, they chose the path of disbelief. Allah's established way is that when He shows the light of Islam to people once, if they appreciate it, He increases their light; but if they do not appreciate it and constantly look back, He deprives them of that light as well and places a seal on their hearts. Consequently, they lose the capacity to think correctly. The reasons and effects of the seal placed on the hearts of the Jews have been detailed in Surah Al-Baqarah; you may refer to it. It is about such people that Jesus (peace be upon him) said that even what they have will be taken away from them.

(And when you see them, their bodies please you, and if they speak, you listen to their speech. They are as if they were pieces of wood propped up. They think every shout is against them. They are the enemy, so beware of them. May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded?) (4).
(A portrait of the hypocrites' outer and inner states): This is a depiction of these hypocrites. If you reflect, you will find that in a few brief sentences, their outer and inner states have been painted in such a way that no aspect remains hidden. The first two sentences portray their outer appearance, while the subsequent two throw light on their inner state. After this, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is urged to remain alert to them, and at the same time, regret is expressed over their condition.
(And when you see them, their bodies please you): It is stated that when you look at them, their well-nourished bodies and polished appearances seem attractive to you. It should be noted that these hypocrites were in good financial standing. First, they had accumulated wealth through various means, both lawful and unlawful. Second, they were extremely miserly, unwilling to spend a single penny on any religious or social cause except for their own personal comfort and luxury. The effect of this material ease was visible on their bodies and appearances. Their leader, the infamous hypocrite Abdullah bin Ubayy, was very wealthy, and the other members of his group were also well-off. (and if they speak, you listen to their speech): That is, they are highly eloquent. When they speak, they lecture in support of and with zeal for Islam in such a way that they captivate the listener's heart, and people start listening to their words. It has been mentioned above that these people assure Allah and His Messenger of their loyalty through their oaths and spellbinding speech. In Surah Muhammad, this characteristic of theirs is also described: they express great enthusiasm for jihad with their tongues, but when the command for jihad was actually given, they began to hide. Since these hypocrites knew that their cowardice was apparent to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the general Muslims, whenever they came to his presence, they tried to convince him with their oratorical skills that they were devotees of Islam, ready to sacrifice everything in its path. Although the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was well aware of these verbal warriors, out of his noble nature, he would listen to their talk, which made them feel reassured that their spellbinding speech had worked.
(They are as if they were pieces of wood propped up): This reflects their inner state. Although their bodies and appearances are attractive, the hearts inside their bodies are dead. As mentioned in the preceding verse, Allah has sealed them. Due to this spiritual and internal death, their example in your assembly is like hollow logs of wood clothed in garments and propped up against the walls.
(They think every shout is against them): The literal meaning of 'sayhah' is a shout, but it also carries the meaning of danger, which is the meaning intended here. That is, although these people wish to convince you of their bravery and readiness for any sacrifice through their eloquence, they are cowards of the highest degree. Whenever any danger appears, they assume out of cowardice that this bolt of lightning is bound to strike them. What can be expected of those whose cowardice is of this level when they actually have to face a real danger? (They are the enemy, so beware of them): That is, they try to cast suspicion on others in your eyes to prove their own innocence, but in reality, they are the true enemies of Islam. Remain alert to them at all times. It should be noted here that one of the tactics of these hypocrites was to blame innocent Muslims for the crimes they themselves committed, so that they might appear innocent before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).
(May Allah destroy them; how are they deluded?): This is both a severe rebuke and an expression of regret over their state. It is said: May Allah ruin them! How have they been turned upside down that their every step is in the wrong direction! This character of the hypocrites is depicted in many places in the Qur'an. For the sake of brevity, we present only one example from Surah Al-Baqarah:
(And of the people is he whose speech pleases you in worldly life, and he calls Allah to witness as to what is in his heart, yet he is the fiercest of opponents. And when he turns away, he strives throughout the land to cause corruption therein and destroy crops and animals. And Allah does not love corruption. And when it is said to him, 'Fear Allah,' pride in the sin takes hold of him. Sufficient for him is Hell, and wretched is the resting place.) (Al-Baqarah, 2:204-206).

(And when it is said to them, 'Come, the Messenger of Allah will ask forgiveness for you,' they turn their heads aside and you see them evading while they are arrogant) (5).
(False self-pride): This states the same reality in slightly different words as expressed in the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah cited above: 'And when it is said to him, "Fear Allah," pride in the sin takes hold of him.' The meaning is: when their conspiracies and mischief are noticed and they are asked to present themselves before the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and repent, so that he may ask forgiveness for them and his prayer may serve as an intercession for them with Allah, they puff up with pride, shake their heads, and turn away with extreme arrogance.
Their haughty avoidance as expressed through their physical gestures is described by the words 'they turn their heads aside' (lawwaw ru'usahum), which is a highly eloquent description. The inner effect of this attitude is described by the words 'they evade while they are arrogant' (yasudduna wa hum mustakbirun). Thus, both their outer and inner states are laid bare. It should be noted that those who lack moral courage do not admit their faults or express regret even after their sins are exposed. They fear that if they confess their crime once, their reputation will be lost forever. This weakness keeps them bound to their sins. This characteristic is described in the verse of Surah Al-Baqarah by the words: 'pride in the sin takes hold of him'.

(It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; never will Allah forgive them. Indeed, Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people) (6).
(Those who persist in sins are deprived of forgiveness): The punishment for their pride and avoidance is that Allah addressed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and issued this decree concerning them: whether you pray for their forgiveness or not, Allah will never forgive them. Allah does not bless with His guidance those who persist in disobedience. He only guides those who feel remorse for their sins and turn back to Allah and His Messenger. In Surah An-Nisa, this theme is described as follows:
(And if, when they wronged themselves, they had come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful.) (An-Nisa, 4:64).
Regarding those who behave arrogantly toward Allah and His Messenger, this decree was issued in Surah At-Tawbah:
(Ask forgiveness for them, [O Muhammad], or do not ask forgiveness for them. If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times—never will Allah forgive them.) (At-Tawbah, 9:80).

(They are the ones who say, 'Do not spend on those who are with the Messenger of Allah until they disband.' And to Allah belong the depositories of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand) (7).
(The cause of Allah's wrath): This explains why these people deserved the wrath of Allah to the extent that even if the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) asks forgiveness for them seventy times, Allah will never forgive them. It is stated: these are the very people who prevent others, saying, 'Do not spend on the companions of the Messenger of Allah so that they may disband.'
Narrations state that in 6 AH, during the Expedition of Banu al-Mustaliq, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) camped near a well, where a dispute arose over water between a poor Emigrant (Muhajir) and a Helper (Ansari). The Emigrant struck the Ansari, who then called upon the Ansar for help, while the Emigrant called upon the Muhajirun. Men from both sides drew their swords and gathered. Although, by Allah's grace, the matter did not escalate further, Abdullah bin Ubayy—who was always looking for such opportunities—exploited the situation to incite the emotions of the Ansar against the Muhajirun, uttering highly poisonous words. He said: 'They have found shelter in our land and now they are growling at us. True is the saying: "Fatten your dog and it will bite you." By Allah, when we return, the more honorable will surely expel therefrom the more mean.'
Addressing the Ansar who had gathered around him during this incident, he said: 'This is the consequence of your own mistake. You welcomed them into your homes and shared your wealth with them. By Allah, if you withhold your support from them, they would have fled from here long ago.'
The verse points to some of these very statements of his, indicating: how can Allah forgive those whose hearts are filled with such hatred and envy against Islam and the Muslims?
(The response to Abdullah bin Ubayy's taunts): (And to Allah belong the depositories of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand) - This is the response to Abdullah bin Ubayy's statement. This hypocrite assumes that if they withhold their support from the Emigrants, the latter will have no sustainers left, whereas all the treasures of the heavens and the earth belong to Allah alone. He gives to whom He wills and takes away from whom He wills, but since the hearts of these hypocrites have been sealed, they are unable to understand this reality.

(They say, 'If we return to Medina, the more honorable will surely expel therefrom the more mean.' And to Allah belongs [all] honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know) (8).
This refers to the second poisonous statement of Abdullah bin Ubayy mentioned above. To incite the Ansar against the Muhajirun, he said: 'Now, when we return to Medina, the first thing to do is that those possessing honor and power (meaning the Ansar) will expel from Medina those who are mean' (referring to the Muhajirun). Thus, he raised the same pre-Islamic slogan that was characteristic of the Arabs—claiming that they had always been the honorable and powerful ones in this land, and that this was their exclusive right, questioning how they could tolerate those who sought shelter with them striking their men.
(And to Allah belongs honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know): This is the response to that pre-Islamic slogan: true honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers, and according to the divine law, the time for its manifestation has now arrived, but the hypocrites do not know this. A reference to this divine law has appeared in Surah Al-Mujadilah in these words:
(Indeed, those who oppose Allah and His Messenger—those will be among the most humbled. Allah has written, 'I will surely overcome, I and My messengers.' Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.) (Al-Mujadilah, 58:20-21).

(O you who have believed, let not your wealth and your children divert you from the remembrance of Allah. And whoever does that—then those are the losers) (9).
(Warning to the Muslims): Finally, the Muslims are warned: do not imitate the way of these hypocrites. Love for wealth and children has made them heedless of God, leaving them with no desire to prepare for the Hereafter. If you, like them, become entangled in the love of wealth and children and forget God and the Hereafter, remember that this is the ultimate ruin and deprivation. Those who forget Allah end up forgetting their own destiny and fall into the hands of Satan. In Surah Al-Mujadilah, it is said concerning the hypocrites: (Satan has overcome them and made them forget the remembrance of Allah. Those are the party of Satan. Unquestionably, the party of Satan—they are the losers) (Al-Mujadilah, 58:19).

(And spend [in the way of Allah] from what We have provided you before death comes to one of you and he says, 'My Lord, if only You would delay me for a brief term so I would give charity and be among the righteous') (10).
This points out the way to keep the remembrance of Allah alive, avoid the trial of wealth and children, and remain secure from the onset of hypocrisy: every person must spend out of the sustenance Allah has given them to make earnings for the Hereafter before death arrives. Let it not be that when death suddenly approaches, they say with regret: 'My Lord, why did You not grant me a little more respite so that I could give charity? If I could have done so, I would have been among the righteous.'
In Surah At-Tawbah, this aspect of the hypocrites' character is highlighted—that they keep their hands closed due to miserliness. When they were poor, they used to express their desire and vow everywhere, saying: 'If Allah improves our condition, we will spend generously in His way, perform great services for the religion, and join the ranks of the righteous.' However, when Allah fulfilled their desire out of His grace, they turned back and walked away. As a punishment for this miserliness, God implanted hypocrisy in their hearts, depriving them of the opportunity He had granted them to give charity and become of the righteous. It is stated:
(And among them are those who made a covenant with Allah, [saying], 'If He should give us of His bounty, we will surely give charity and be among the righteous.' But when He gave them of His bounty, they were stingy with it and turned away while they were averse. So He penalized them with hypocrisy in their hearts...) (At-Tawbah, 9:75-77).
We have clarified in the commentary of Surah Al-Hadid that those who avoid spending in the way of Allah do not merely distinguish themselves from the righteous believers by being tainted with hypocrisy in this world, but will also face its consequences in the Hereafter in a way that will be a source of deep regret for them. In the Hereafter, Allah will raise those men and women who gave charity in this world alongside the ranks of the righteous, which include the truthful (Siddiqeen) and the witnesses (Shuhada), and their light will guide them toward Paradise. The hypocritical men and women will be deprived of this light; a wall will be placed between them and the righteous, and it will be made clear to them that the condition for joining the ranks of the righteous was spending and giving charity, which they avoided in the world. In this verse, 'akun' (which is the jussive form of the present tense) is apparently linked to 'fa-assaddaqa' (which is subjunctive), but in our view, 'akun min as-salihin' (be among the righteous) is actually the response to an omitted conditional clause. If unpacked, the meaning is: 'if I were able to give this charity, I would have become of the righteous.' Since the indication for this condition was clear in 'fa-assaddaqa', the condition was omitted.

(But never will Allah delay a soul when its time has come. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do) (11).
That is, when the hour of death arrives, this kind of regret will remain nothing but regret; it will be of no benefit. Once the opportunity of life has passed, Allah never grants any respite to anyone. Moreover, Allah knows well that the vow you are making today to spend and lead a righteous life is completely hollow. Even if you were granted further respite, you would only do what you have been doing up to now. With the help of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. (All praise and favor belong to Allah). March 30, 1978 / Rabi' al-Thani 19, 1398 AH.
